description_long: This object is a sculpted guard stone resembling an armadillo, crafted from a pale, weathered stone material. The surface is textured with detailed ridges and scales, mimicking the segmented armor of an armadillo, and the form is rounded and squat, with a pronounced head and tail. The stone shows signs of age, with some discoloration and minor surface erosion, particularly along the ridges and base. The object sits on a rectangular slab that appears to be part of its installation, likely to stabilize it on the ground. Its coloration is predominantly a light grayish-white, with darker patches from accumulated dirt and exposure to the elements. The overall impression is of a robust, decorative barrier designed to prevent vehicle encroachment or mark boundaries in an urban or park setting.
description: A robust, armadillo-shaped stone guard stone with detailed ridges and a weathered, pale surface, mounted on a rectangular base. Its textured exterior and squat form make it a distinctive urban barrier.
description_view_0: Front view shows the armadillo's head and snout, with pronounced facial features and the start of the segmented armor.
description_view_1: Side view highlights the curved back, segmented ridges, and the transition from head to tail, emphasizing the object's length.
description_view_2: Rear view displays the rounded tail end and the full width of the back, with visible texture and weathering.
description_view_3: Opposite side view mirrors view 1, showing the other flank and the detailed segmentation of the stone surface.
attribute_location: Typically placed at the entrance to driveways, along sidewalks, or at the corners of urban parks to prevent vehicle access and protect pedestrian areas.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be fully above ground, resting flush on a paved or compacted surface, sometimes partially embedded for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible surface discoloration and minor erosion along the ridges and base, likely caused by weather exposure, occasional vehicle contact, and foot traffic. The top and sides show smoothing from repeated contact, while the base has accumulated dirt and grime.
